---
title: "Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"    # data points / generic vectors
ARROW  = "#c1121f"    # the highlighted arrow / first basis direction
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights / second orthogonal direction
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PURPLE = "#6a4c93"    # third direction / complement
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fifth article in the **Geometry of Data**
> track and the second half of its length-and-angle movement. It assumes
> [GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension*] (a basis is a minimal spanning set;
> dimension is the invariant count of directions a subspace has) and, above all,
> [GEOM-04 *The Dot Product: Length and Angle*], which gave us the dot product
> $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = \sum_i x_i y_i$, the norm $\lVert\mathbf x\rVert =
> \sqrt{\mathbf x^\top\mathbf x}$ as length, and the angle relation
> $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert\lVert\mathbf y\rVert\cos\theta$. Here we
> follow the single most consequential special case of that angle relation — $\theta =
> 90^\circ$, where the dot product is zero — all the way to the bottom. It sets up the
> keystone [GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*] (the split this article proves
> *exists and is unique* is the object GEOM-06 computes), [GEOM-07 *Gram–Schmidt and QR*]
> (the machine that manufactures the orthonormal bases we praise here), and
> [GEOM-16 *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*] (whose payoff is an orthonormal
> eigenbasis — the best of both worlds).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Two vectors are **orthogonal** when their dot product is zero — the coordinate-free
fingerprint of a right angle, valid in any number of dimensions. This article takes that
one equation, $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 0$, and follows it to three destinations the
rest of the series leans on. First, the **generalized Pythagorean theorem**: when
vectors are orthogonal their squared lengths simply add, $\lVert\mathbf
x+\mathbf y\rVert^2 = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf y\rVert^2$ — derived, not
asserted. Second, the **orthonormal basis**: a basis of mutually perpendicular unit
vectors, in which the coordinate of any vector along a basis direction is *just a dot
product*, $c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$ — no linear system to solve, ever. Third, the
**orthogonal complement** and the **orthogonal decomposition theorem**: every vector
splits in exactly one way into a part lying in a chosen subspace and a part perpendicular
to it. The single claim this article defends is that **orthogonality turns geometry into
arithmetic** — angles, lengths, coordinates, and the all-important "nearest point in a
subspace" computation all collapse into dot products the moment a basis is orthonormal.
That collapse is why orthonormal bases are the coordinate systems every numerical method
in this series secretly wants to be working in.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
**Orthogonal** means dot product zero: $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 0$ is the
coordinate-free statement of "at right angles," and it makes sense in $\mathbb R^n$ for
any $n$, far past anything you can draw. Two consequences make orthogonality the most
useful relationship in linear algebra. (1) Perpendicular pieces **don't interfere**: their
squared lengths add (Pythagoras), so a vector's total energy is the sum of its energies
along orthogonal directions. (2) In an **orthonormal basis** — perpendicular unit vectors
— finding a vector's coordinates stops being a linear system and becomes a list of dot
products, $c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$. Everything else in this article, and the
projection machinery of the entire series, is built from those two facts.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. Hold onto one image: an orthonormal basis is a set of
perfectly square, perfectly aligned measuring sticks. Reading a vector's coordinates is
then no harder than reading a ruler — you lay each stick alongside the vector and the dot
product tells you how far it reaches. The moment the sticks are skewed or unequal (a
general basis), reading a coordinate requires *untangling* the others, which is a linear
system. The whole article is the story of why "perpendicular and unit length" buys you
out of that entanglement.

# Why we are here: the tyranny of a skewed grid {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why do we go to the trouble of
demanding that a basis be orthonormal, when any basis already describes the space
perfectly well?** Every derivation below is, at bottom, an answer to that question.

## What came before, and why it was not enough

By [GEOM-03] we already have everything we *need* to describe a subspace: pick a basis —
any linearly independent spanning set — and every vector in the space has a unique list of
coordinates relative to it. That sounds like the end of the story. It is not, and the
reason is purely practical.

Suppose your basis is $\{\mathbf v_1,\mathbf v_2\}$ in the plane, and you are handed a
vector $\mathbf x$. Its coordinates $(c_1, c_2)$ are the unique numbers with
$\mathbf x = c_1\mathbf v_1 + c_2\mathbf v_2$. To *find* them you must solve that vector
equation — two scalar equations in the two unknowns $c_1, c_2$. With a general basis the
equations are coupled: $c_1$ cannot be read off without simultaneously pinning down $c_2$,
because $\mathbf v_1$ has a "shadow" along $\mathbf v_2$ and vice versa. Solve a $2\times2$
system every time you want a coordinate; in $n$ dimensions, an $n\times n$ system.

This is the tyranny of a skewed grid. Think of a sheet of graph paper that someone has
sheared sideways and stretched unevenly: to say where a point is, you cannot just glance
along one axis, because moving along one ruler also drags you along the other. The grid
*works*, but it fights you. @fig-skewed-vs-square makes the contrast concrete.

```{python}
#| label: fig-skewed-vs-square
#| fig-cap: "Two coordinate systems for the same point $\\mathbf{x}$ (black). **Left:** a skewed, unequal basis $\\{\\mathbf{v}_1,\\mathbf{v}_2\\}$. To find the coordinates you must solve a coupled $2\\times2$ system — the dashed construction lines that locate $\\mathbf{x}$ on this grid are not perpendicular to the axes, so neither coordinate can be read in isolation. **Right:** an orthonormal basis $\\{\\mathbf{q}_1,\\mathbf{q}_2\\}$. The construction lines *are* perpendicular drops onto each axis, so each coordinate is a single, independent measurement — exactly the dot product $\\mathbf{q}_i^\\top\\mathbf{x}$ we derive in @sec-coords."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

# ---- Left: skewed basis ----
v1 = np.array([1.6, 0.4])
v2 = np.array([0.5, 1.3])
# target point in skewed coords (c1, c2) = (1.4, 1.1)
c1, c2 = 1.4, 1.1
x = c1 * v1 + c2 * v2
# draw skewed grid
for c in np.arange(-1, 4):
    axL.plot(*np.array([c*v1 - 1*v2, c*v1 + 3*v2]).T, color=GREY, lw=0.7, alpha=0.5)
    axL.plot(*np.array([-1*v1 + c*v2, 3*v1 + c*v2]).T, color=GREY, lw=0.7, alpha=0.5)
axL.annotate("", xy=v1, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.2))
axL.annotate("", xy=v2, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2))
axL.plot([c1*v1[0], x[0]], [c1*v1[1], x[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axL.plot([c2*v2[0], x[0]], [c2*v2[1], x[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axL.scatter(*x, color="black", s=60, zorder=5)
axL.text(*(v1 + [0.05, -0.18]), r"$\mathbf{v}_1$", color=ARROW, fontsize=12)
axL.text(*(v2 + [-0.32, 0.02]), r"$\mathbf{v}_2$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
axL.text(*(x + [0.08, 0.05]), r"$\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=12)
axL.set_title("Skewed basis: coordinates are coupled")
axL.set_xlim(-0.5, 3.6); axL.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.0); axL.set_aspect("equal")

# ---- Right: orthonormal basis ----
q1 = np.array([1.0, 0.0])
q2 = np.array([0.0, 1.0])
xo = np.array([2.2, 1.6])
for c in np.arange(-1, 5):
    axR.axvline(c, color=GREY, lw=0.7, alpha=0.5)
    axR.axhline(c, color=GREY, lw=0.7, alpha=0.5)
axR.annotate("", xy=q1, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.2))
axR.annotate("", xy=q2, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2))
axR.plot([xo[0], xo[0]], [0, xo[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axR.plot([0, xo[0]], [xo[1], xo[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axR.scatter(*xo, color="black", s=60, zorder=5)
axR.text(*(q1 + [0.02, -0.22]), r"$\mathbf{q}_1$", color=ARROW, fontsize=12)
axR.text(*(q2 + [-0.34, 0.0]), r"$\mathbf{q}_2$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
axR.text(*(xo + [0.08, 0.05]), r"$\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=12)
axR.set_title("Orthonormal basis: read each coordinate alone")
axR.set_xlim(-0.5, 3.6); axR.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.0); axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The fix, and the historical thread

The fix is to insist the axes be **mutually perpendicular and of unit length** — an
*orthonormal* basis. Then the cross-talk vanishes: moving along one axis does not move you
along any other, so each coordinate becomes an independent measurement. That is exactly
the Cartesian coordinate system Descartes drew in 1637 — perpendicular axes, equal units —
and the reason it became the default picture of space is precisely the one we are about to
make rigorous: it is the grid in which measurement is trivial.

The deeper historical engine is the same one that drove [REGR-01] and will drive the
keystone [GEOM-06]: **Fourier**, in the early 1800s, found that a complicated periodic
signal could be written as a sum of sines and cosines, and that the coefficient of each
sine was obtained by a single integral — a continuous dot product — *against that sine
alone*, with no interference from the others. That miracle ("just integrate against the
basis function to get its coefficient") is nothing but the orthonormal-coordinate formula
$c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$ in an infinite-dimensional space. Sines and cosines are
orthogonal. The entire edifice of Fourier analysis, signal processing, and the
eigenbases of [GEOM-16] rests on the small, sturdy fact that orthogonal directions do not
interfere. We are building that fact from the floor.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just always use the standard basis $\mathbf e_1,\dots,\mathbf e_n$? It is already
orthonormal."* For describing all of $\mathbb R^n$, you often can. But two situations force
a *different* orthonormal basis on you. (1) You are working **inside a subspace** — a plane
in $\mathbb R^3$, the column space of a data matrix in $\mathbb R^n$ — and the standard
axes do not lie in it; you need an orthonormal basis *of that subspace*, which Gram–Schmidt
([GEOM-07]) builds. (2) The natural directions of a problem are not the coordinate axes:
the principal axes of a data cloud, the eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix ([GEOM-16]). In
both cases we want the *computational comfort* of an orthonormal basis aligned with the
geometry of the problem, not with the arbitrary axes the data happened to be recorded in.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Two pictures carry the entire article; meet them before any algebra.

**Picture one: perpendicular pieces do not interfere.** Walk 3 metres east, then 4 metres
north. How far are you from the start? Not 7 — the directions are perpendicular, so the
trip east contributes *nothing* to your northward progress and vice versa. The distances
combine through the hypotenuse: $\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$. That is the Pythagorean theorem, and
its real content is independence of perpendicular directions: each axis carries its own
displacement, and the squared total is the sum of the squared parts. Replace "east/north"
with "any two orthogonal vectors in $\mathbb R^n$" and the same statement holds — that is
@sec-pythagoras.

**Picture two: a coordinate is a shadow.** Stand a vector $\mathbf x$ up and shine a light
straight down onto a unit axis $\mathbf q$. The length of the shadow $\mathbf x$ casts on
that axis is how far $\mathbf x$ reaches in the $\mathbf q$ direction — its coordinate. From
[GEOM-04], the length of that shadow is exactly $\mathbf q^\top\mathbf x$ (because
$\mathbf q^\top\mathbf x = \lVert\mathbf q\rVert\lVert\mathbf x\rVert\cos\theta =
\lVert\mathbf x\rVert\cos\theta$ when $\mathbf q$ is a unit vector, and
$\lVert\mathbf x\rVert\cos\theta$ is precisely the adjacent side — the shadow length). When
the axes are mutually perpendicular, the shadows do not contaminate one another: the shadow
on $\mathbf q_1$ ignores $\mathbf q_2$ entirely. So we can collect all the coordinates one
shadow at a time, independently. That is the whole reason @sec-coords works.

@fig-shadow shows the shadow picture for a single axis; keep it in mind, because the
coordinate formula and the projection of [GEOM-06] are both just "measure the shadow."

```{python}
#| label: fig-shadow
#| fig-cap: "A coordinate is the length of a shadow. The vector $\\mathbf{x}$ (black) casts a perpendicular shadow onto the unit direction $\\mathbf{q}$ (red). The shadow's signed length is $\\mathbf{q}^\\top\\mathbf{x} = \\lVert\\mathbf{x}\\rVert\\cos\\theta$ — this single number is $\\mathbf{x}$'s coordinate along $\\mathbf{q}$. The dashed segment, from the tip of $\\mathbf{x}$ down to the axis, meets $\\mathbf{q}$ at a right angle: that perpendicularity is what makes the shadow the *correct* measure of reach, and it is the seed of orthogonal projection (GEOM-06)."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 4.2))
q = np.array([1.0, 0.0])
x = np.array([2.3, 1.7])
proj_len = q @ x
foot = proj_len * q
ax.annotate("", xy=3.2*q, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.0))
ax.annotate("", xy=x, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color="black", lw=2.2))
ax.plot([x[0], foot[0]], [x[1], foot[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.scatter(*foot, color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=5)
# right-angle marker
r = 0.16
ax.plot([foot[0]-r, foot[0]-r, foot[0]], [foot[1], foot[1]+r, foot[1]+r], color=GREY, lw=1.1)
ax.text(*(x + [0.05, 0.05]), r"$\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=13)
ax.text(2.7, -0.22, r"$\mathbf{q}$", color=ARROW, fontsize=13)
ax.text(foot[0]-0.05, -0.32, r"$\mathbf{q}^\top\mathbf{x}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.annotate(r"$\theta$", xy=(0.45, 0.12), fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-0.4, 3.4); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 2.2); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("The coordinate of $\\mathbf{x}$ along $\\mathbf{q}$ is its shadow")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We build in four stages: define orthogonality, prove it forces linear independence,
prove the Pythagorean theorem, then define orthonormal bases and harvest the
coordinate formula. The final stage — the orthogonal complement and the decomposition
theorem — is the bridge to the keystone.

## Orthogonality, defined {#sec-def-orth}

::: {#def-orthogonal}
**Definition (orthogonal vectors).** Two vectors $\mathbf x,\mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n$ are
**orthogonal**, written $\mathbf x\perp\mathbf y$, when their dot product is zero:
$$
\mathbf x \perp \mathbf y \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \mathbf x^\top\mathbf y
= \sum_{i=1}^n x_i y_i = 0 .
$$ {#eq-def-orth}
:::

Two things deserve immediate comment, because they are exactly the questions a careful
reader raises.

First, **why is this the right definition of "perpendicular"?** It is not a fresh
assumption — it is forced by the angle relation we already proved in [GEOM-04],
$$
\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf y\rVert\cos\theta ,
$$ {#eq-angle}
where $\theta\in[0,\pi]$ is the angle between the two arrows. For nonzero vectors,
$\lVert\mathbf x\rVert>0$ and $\lVert\mathbf y\rVert>0$, so the right-hand side of
@eq-angle is zero **exactly when** $\cos\theta = 0$, i.e. exactly when $\theta = 90^\circ$.
So @eq-def-orth says nothing more nor less than "the angle between them is a right angle."
The merit of writing it as $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 0$ rather than "$\theta = 90^\circ$"
is that the dot product is a finite sum of products of coordinates — it is *computable* and
makes sense in $\mathbb R^{100}$, where "the angle looks like $90^\circ$" means nothing
visual. Orthogonality is how we *do* right angles in dimensions we cannot see.

Second, **the zero vector is orthogonal to everything**, including itself: if
$\mathbf x = \mathbf 0$ then $\mathbf 0^\top\mathbf y = \sum_i 0\cdot y_i = 0$ for every
$\mathbf y$. This is a harmless edge convention — $\mathbf 0$ has no direction, so it can be
declared "at right angles" to all directions without contradiction — but we flag it because
one theorem below will need to *exclude* the zero vector explicitly.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is orthogonality the same as linear independence?"* No — and confusing them is a classic
slip. Orthogonality is *stronger*. Orthogonal (nonzero) vectors are always linearly
independent (we prove this next, in @thm-orth-indep), but independent vectors need not be
orthogonal — the skewed basis $\{\mathbf v_1,\mathbf v_2\}$ of @fig-skewed-vs-square is
independent yet plainly not perpendicular. Independence says "none is a combination of the
others"; orthogonality says "they meet at right angles." Every orthogonal set is
independent; the converse fails. The whole point of Gram–Schmidt ([GEOM-07]) is to *upgrade*
a merely-independent basis into an orthonormal one.
:::

## Orthogonal sets are automatically independent {#sec-orth-indep}

We jumped ahead and claimed orthogonality implies independence. We owe a proof. First the
object.

::: {#def-orth-set}
**Definition (orthogonal set).** A set of vectors $\{\mathbf u_1,\dots,\mathbf u_k\}$ in
$\mathbb R^n$ is an **orthogonal set** if every distinct pair is orthogonal:
$\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j = 0$ whenever $i\neq j$. If in addition every vector has unit
length, $\lVert\mathbf u_i\rVert = 1$ for all $i$, the set is **orthonormal**.
:::

A compact way to write both conditions at once uses the **Kronecker delta** $\delta_{ij}$,
which is $1$ when $i=j$ and $0$ when $i\neq j$. An orthonormal set is exactly one with
$$
\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j = \delta_{ij}
= \begin{cases} 1 & i = j,\\ 0 & i\neq j.\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-kron}
The diagonal case $i=j$ reads $\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_i = \lVert\mathbf u_i\rVert^2 = 1$
(unit length), and the off-diagonal case $i\neq j$ reads $\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j = 0$
(mutual orthogonality). One equation, both requirements. We will use @eq-kron constantly.

::: {#thm-orth-indep}
**Theorem (orthogonal $\Rightarrow$ independent).** Any orthogonal set of **nonzero**
vectors $\{\mathbf u_1,\dots,\mathbf u_k\}$ is linearly independent.
:::

::: {.proof}
By the definition of linear independence from [GEOM-03], we must show that the only way to
combine the vectors into the zero vector is with all-zero coefficients. So suppose some
scalars $c_1,\dots,c_k$ satisfy
$$
c_1\mathbf u_1 + c_2\mathbf u_2 + \dots + c_k\mathbf u_k = \mathbf 0 .
$$
We will show every $c_j$ must be $0$. The trick is the entire moral of the article: **probe
the equation by dotting it with one basis vector at a time, and orthogonality kills every
term but one.** Fix any index $j$ and take the dot product of both sides with $\mathbf u_j$.
The dot product is linear in its second argument (proved in [GEOM-04]:
$\mathbf u_j^\top(\mathbf a + \mathbf b) = \mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf a + \mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf b$
and $\mathbf u_j^\top(c\,\mathbf a) = c\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf a$), so it distributes across
the sum:
$$
\mathbf u_j^\top\bigl(c_1\mathbf u_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf u_k\bigr)
= c_1\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_1 + c_2\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_2 + \dots
+ c_k\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_k
= \mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf 0 = 0 .
$$
Now invoke orthogonality. In that sum, every term $c_i\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_i$ with
$i\neq j$ has $\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_i = 0$ and vanishes. The *only* survivor is the
$i=j$ term, $c_j\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_j$. So the whole equation collapses to
$$
c_j\,\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_j = 0,
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
c_j\,\lVert\mathbf u_j\rVert^2 = 0 ,
$$
using $\mathbf u_j^\top\mathbf u_j = \lVert\mathbf u_j\rVert^2$ from [GEOM-04]. Here is where
we spend the *nonzero* hypothesis: $\mathbf u_j\neq\mathbf 0$ means
$\lVert\mathbf u_j\rVert^2 > 0$, so we may divide by it, forcing $c_j = 0$. The index $j$
was arbitrary, so $c_1 = c_2 = \dots = c_k = 0$. The only combination giving $\mathbf 0$ is
the trivial one, which is precisely linear independence. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This little proof is worth more than its modest theorem. The move "dot the equation with
$\mathbf u_j$ and watch all but one term die" is the engine of the coordinate formula
(@sec-coords), the projection formula (GEOM-06), and Fourier coefficients alike. Learn it
once here; you will see it for the rest of the series.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You said orthogonal vectors are independent. Does the converse hold — must independent
vectors be orthogonal?"* No, and the asymmetry is the reason orthonormal bases are special
rather than automatic. There are infinitely many independent bases of a plane, but only
the perpendicular-unit ones earn the computational gifts below. @thm-orth-indep is a
one-way street: orthogonality is a *bonus structure* on top of independence, and most
bases do not have it until you manufacture it (Gram–Schmidt, [GEOM-07]).
:::

## The generalized Pythagorean theorem {#sec-pythagoras}

We now prove "picture one": perpendicular pieces do not interfere, so their squared
lengths add. This is the engine behind decompositions of variance, the bias–variance
identity, and the least-squares residual bookkeeping, and it is a two-line consequence of
expanding a norm.

::: {#thm-pythagoras}
**Theorem (Pythagoras in $\mathbb R^n$).** If $\mathbf x\perp\mathbf y$ then
$$
\lVert\mathbf x + \mathbf y\rVert^2 = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf y\rVert^2 .
$$ {#eq-pythagoras}
More generally, if $\{\mathbf u_1,\dots,\mathbf u_k\}$ is an orthogonal set then
$$
\Bigl\lVert \sum_{i=1}^k \mathbf u_i \Bigr\rVert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k \lVert\mathbf u_i\rVert^2 .
$$ {#eq-pythagoras-general}
:::

::: {.proof}
Start from the definition of the norm via the dot product, $\lVert\mathbf v\rVert^2 =
\mathbf v^\top\mathbf v$ (this *is* the norm, from [GEOM-04]), applied to
$\mathbf v = \mathbf x + \mathbf y$. The dot product distributes over addition in each slot
— exactly the FOIL expansion, valid because $\mathbf a^\top\mathbf b$ is linear in both
arguments (GEOM-04):
$$
\lVert\mathbf x + \mathbf y\rVert^2
= (\mathbf x + \mathbf y)^\top(\mathbf x + \mathbf y)
= \mathbf x^\top\mathbf x + \mathbf x^\top\mathbf y + \mathbf y^\top\mathbf x
+ \mathbf y^\top\mathbf y .
$$
The dot product is **symmetric**, $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = \mathbf y^\top\mathbf x$
(both equal $\sum_i x_i y_i$; proved in GEOM-04), so the two cross terms are equal and
combine:
$$
\lVert\mathbf x + \mathbf y\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 + 2\,\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y + \lVert\mathbf y\rVert^2 .
$$ {#eq-norm-expand}
This identity holds for *any* two vectors — it is the law of cosines in disguise, since
$2\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 2\lVert\mathbf x\rVert\lVert\mathbf y\rVert\cos\theta$ by
@eq-angle. Now impose orthogonality: $\mathbf x\perp\mathbf y$ means
$\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 0$, so the entire cross term disappears, leaving
$\lVert\mathbf x+\mathbf y\rVert^2 = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf y\rVert^2$,
which is @eq-pythagoras.

For the general case @eq-pythagoras-general, expand the squared norm of the sum the same
way. Writing $\mathbf s = \sum_{i} \mathbf u_i$,
$$
\Bigl\lVert\sum_{i=1}^k\mathbf u_i\Bigr\rVert^2
= \Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^k\mathbf u_i\Bigr)^{\!\top}\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^k\mathbf u_j\Bigr)
= \sum_{i=1}^k\sum_{j=1}^k \mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j ,
$$
where the last step multiplies out the two sums into all $k^2$ pairwise dot products
(distributivity again). Split that double sum into its diagonal terms ($i = j$) and its
off-diagonal terms ($i\neq j$):
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k\sum_{j=1}^k \mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j
= \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^k \mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_i}_{i=j}
+ \underbrace{\sum_{i\neq j}\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j}_{i\neq j} .
$$
By orthogonality every off-diagonal $\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j$ (with $i\neq j$) is zero,
so the second sum is $0$, and each diagonal term is
$\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_i = \lVert\mathbf u_i\rVert^2$. What remains is
$\sum_{i=1}^k \lVert\mathbf u_i\rVert^2$, which is @eq-pythagoras-general.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Equation @eq-norm-expand is worth keeping in its own right: it says the squared length of a
sum is the sum of the squared lengths *plus a cross term* $2\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y$, and
**orthogonality is exactly the condition that erases the cross term**. The picture is in
@fig-pythagoras: when the legs are perpendicular, the hypotenuse closes the right triangle
and the classical $a^2+b^2=c^2$ is recovered; when they are not, the cross term measures
how far from a right angle we are.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pythagoras
#| fig-cap: "The generalized Pythagorean theorem, drawn. **Left:** $\\mathbf{x}\\perp\\mathbf{y}$. The sum $\\mathbf{x}+\\mathbf{y}$ (black) is the hypotenuse of a right triangle, and $\\lVert\\mathbf{x}+\\mathbf{y}\\rVert^2=\\lVert\\mathbf{x}\\rVert^2+\\lVert\\mathbf{y}\\rVert^2$ holds exactly. **Right:** when the angle is *not* $90^\\circ$, the triangle is no longer right; the squared hypotenuse picks up the cross term $2\\mathbf{x}^\\top\\mathbf{y}$ of @eq-norm-expand, which is positive for an acute angle (the hypotenuse is longer than Pythagoras would predict)."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))

def draw_triangle(ax, x, y, title):
    s = x + y
    ax.annotate("", xy=x, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.2))
    ax.annotate("", xy=s, xytext=x, arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2))
    ax.annotate("", xy=s, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color="black", lw=2.4))
    ax.text(*(x*0.5 + [0.0, -0.28]), r"$\mathbf{x}$", color=ARROW, fontsize=13)
    ax.text(*(x + y*0.5 + [0.1, 0.0]), r"$\mathbf{y}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
    ax.text(*(s*0.5 + [-0.45, 0.18]), r"$\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}$", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")

x = np.array([3.0, 0.0]); y = np.array([0.0, 2.0])
draw_triangle(axL, x, y, "Orthogonal: $a^2+b^2=c^2$")
# right-angle marker
axL.plot([3.0-0.22, 3.0-0.22, 3.0], [0, 0.22, 0.22], color=GREY, lw=1.1)
axL.set_xlim(-0.4, 3.4); axL.set_ylim(-0.4, 2.4)

x2 = np.array([3.0, 0.0]); y2 = np.array([-1.1, 2.0])
draw_triangle(axR, x2, y2, "Not orthogonal: cross term appears")
axR.set_xlim(-0.6, 3.4); axR.set_ylim(-0.4, 2.4)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does this Pythagorean identity actually show up in data science?"* Constantly,
usually disguised. In least squares ([REGR-04], [GEOM-06]) the data vector splits into a
fitted part and an orthogonal residual part, and @eq-pythagoras becomes the **decomposition
of total sum of squares** $\text{TSS} = \text{ESS} + \text{RSS}$ — the $R^2$ identity is
Pythagoras. In probability, the variance of a sum of *uncorrelated* random variables is the
sum of their variances; "uncorrelated" is orthogonality in the inner-product space of
mean-zero random variables, and that variance identity is again @eq-pythagoras-general. The
bias–variance decomposition is a third instance. Each time, the headline is the same:
*orthogonal contributions add in squared magnitude*.
:::

## Orthonormal bases and the coordinate-as-dot-product formula {#sec-coords}

Now the payoff promised in the Core. An **orthonormal basis** of a subspace $W$ is a basis
of $W$ that is also an orthonormal set: a maximal collection of perpendicular unit vectors
spanning $W$. (By @thm-orth-indep the orthonormality already guarantees independence, so
"orthonormal set that spans $W$" automatically *is* a basis — we get independence for
free.) The reason these are the golden coordinate systems is the following formula.

::: {#thm-onb-coords}
**Theorem (coordinates in an orthonormal basis).** Let $\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_k\}$
be an orthonormal basis of a subspace $W$, and let $\mathbf x\in W$. Then the unique
coordinates of $\mathbf x$ in this basis are dot products:
$$
\mathbf x = \sum_{i=1}^k c_i\,\mathbf q_i,
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x .
$$ {#eq-onb-coords}
:::

::: {.proof}
Because $\{\mathbf q_i\}$ is a basis of $W$ and $\mathbf x\in W$, [GEOM-03] guarantees that
*some* unique coefficients $c_1,\dots,c_k$ exist with $\mathbf x = \sum_{i} c_i\mathbf q_i$.
We do not yet know their values — that is what we are solving for. Use the same probe as in
@thm-orth-indep: dot both sides with a fixed basis vector $\mathbf q_j$ and let
orthonormality flatten the sum. By linearity of the dot product in its second argument,
$$
\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x
= \mathbf q_j^\top\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^k c_i\,\mathbf q_i\Bigr)
= \sum_{i=1}^k c_i\,\bigl(\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i\bigr)
= \sum_{i=1}^k c_i\,\delta_{ji} ,
$$
where the last equality is the orthonormality condition @eq-kron:
$\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i = \delta_{ji}$. Now $\delta_{ji}$ is $0$ for every term except
$i=j$, where it is $1$. So the entire sum collapses to its single surviving term,
$$
\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x = c_j\cdot 1 = c_j .
$$
That is exactly @eq-onb-coords: the $j$-th coordinate is the dot product
$\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x$. Since $j$ was arbitrary, every coordinate is obtained this way.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Stop and appreciate what just happened, because it is the entire reason the word
"orthonormal" carries weight. In a general basis, finding $(c_1,\dots,c_k)$ means
**solving a $k\times k$ linear system** — the coefficients are entangled because the basis
vectors have shadows on each other. In an orthonormal basis the entanglement is gone:
each $c_i$ is read off *independently* with a single dot product, no system, no matrix
inverse, no interference. The cost drops from "solve a system" to "compute $k$ dot
products," and — more importantly for stability ([GEOM-26]) — there is no inverse to be
ill-conditioned. This is the computational gold the Core advertised.

A companion identity falls straight out and is worth stating, because it is Pythagoras
wearing coordinates.

::: {#thm-parseval}
**Theorem (Parseval / length from coordinates).** With $\{\mathbf q_i\}$ orthonormal and
$\mathbf x = \sum_{i} c_i\mathbf q_i$ as in @eq-onb-coords,
$$
\lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k c_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x)^2 .
$$ {#eq-parseval}
:::

::: {.proof}
Apply the general Pythagorean theorem @eq-pythagoras-general to the orthogonal pieces
$\mathbf u_i = c_i\mathbf q_i$ (they are orthogonal because the $\mathbf q_i$ are, and
scaling does not change a right angle):
$$
\lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 = \Bigl\lVert\sum_i c_i\mathbf q_i\Bigr\rVert^2
= \sum_{i=1}^k \lVert c_i\mathbf q_i\rVert^2
= \sum_{i=1}^k c_i^2\,\lVert\mathbf q_i\rVert^2
= \sum_{i=1}^k c_i^2 ,
$$
where $\lVert c_i\mathbf q_i\rVert^2 = c_i^2\lVert\mathbf q_i\rVert^2$ uses the
scaling rule for norms ($\lVert c\mathbf v\rVert = |c|\,\lVert\mathbf v\rVert$, from
GEOM-04), and the final step uses $\lVert\mathbf q_i\rVert = 1$. Substituting
$c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$ gives the right-hand form. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@eq-parseval says the squared length of a vector equals the sum of the squares of its
orthonormal coordinates — the $n$-dimensional Pythagoras for $n$ perpendicular legs. In
Fourier language it is "the energy of a signal equals the sum of the energies of its
frequency components"; in statistics it underlies why an orthonormal change of basis
preserves total variance. It is the same theorem dressed for different occasions.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If I have a matrix $Q$ whose columns are orthonormal, what makes it so convenient?"* Two
facts, both restatements of @eq-kron. Stack the orthonormal vectors as the columns of an
$n\times k$ matrix $Q = [\,\mathbf q_1\ \cdots\ \mathbf q_k\,]$. The $(i,j)$ entry of the
product $Q^\top Q$ is the dot product of column $i$ with column $j$ — i.e.
$\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf q_j = \delta_{ij}$ — so $Q^\top Q = I_k$, the $k\times k$ identity.
That single equation $Q^\top Q = I$ *is* orthonormal columns, and it is why the coordinate
formula @eq-onb-coords reads, in matrix form, $\mathbf c = Q^\top\mathbf x$: applying
$Q^\top$ is reading off all the dot-product coordinates at once. When $Q$ is square
($k=n$) it is called an **orthogonal matrix**, and then $Q^\top Q = I$ also gives
$Q^{-1} = Q^\top$ — the inverse is free, just the transpose. The full matrix-algebra
development of this is [GEOM-10]/[GEOM-11]; here we have derived its geometric content from
the dot product alone.
:::

@fig-onb-3d shows the coordinate formula working in three dimensions, where you can still
just barely see it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-onb-3d
#| fig-cap: "Coordinates as dot products in $\\mathbb R^3$. An orthonormal basis $\\{\\mathbf{q}_1,\\mathbf{q}_2,\\mathbf{q}_3\\}$ (red, green, purple) and a vector $\\mathbf{x}$ (black). Each coordinate $c_i=\\mathbf{q}_i^\\top\\mathbf{x}$ is computed independently — no system to solve — and reconstructs $\\mathbf{x}$ exactly as $c_1\\mathbf{q}_1+c_2\\mathbf{q}_2+c_3\\mathbf{q}_3$. The printout confirms the reconstruction and Parseval's identity $\\lVert\\mathbf{x}\\rVert^2=\\sum c_i^2$ to machine precision."
# Build an orthonormal basis of R^3 via QR of a random matrix (Gram-Schmidt under the hood).
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
Q, _ = np.linalg.qr(rng.standard_normal((3, 3)))
q1, q2, q3 = Q[:, 0], Q[:, 1], Q[:, 2]
x = np.array([1.4, -0.8, 1.1])
c = Q.T @ x                      # coordinates = dot products, all at once

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.2, 5.0))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
O = np.zeros(3)
for v, col, lab in [(q1, ARROW, r"$\mathbf{q}_1$"), (q2, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf{q}_2$"),
                    (q3, PURPLE, r"$\mathbf{q}_3$")]:
    ax.quiver(*O, *v, color=col, lw=2.2, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(*(v*1.12), lab, color=col, fontsize=12)
ax.quiver(*O, *x, color="black", lw=2.6, arrow_length_ratio=0.1)
ax.text(*(x*1.05), r"$\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=13)
ax.set_xlim(-1.3, 1.6); ax.set_ylim(-1.3, 1.6); ax.set_zlim(-1.3, 1.6)
ax.set_xlabel("axis 1"); ax.set_ylabel("axis 2"); ax.set_zlabel("axis 3")
ax.set_title("Each coordinate is one dot product")
ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=35)
plt.tight_layout()

recon = c[0]*q1 + c[1]*q2 + c[2]*q3
print(f"coordinates c = q_i^T x      : {np.round(c, 4)}")
print(f"reconstruction error          : {np.linalg.norm(recon - x):.2e}  (≈ 0)")
print(f"||x||^2                        : {x @ x:.6f}")
print(f"sum c_i^2  (Parseval)          : {c @ c:.6f}")
print(f"Q^T Q (should be identity):\n{np.round(Q.T @ Q, 6)}")
```

## The orthogonal complement and the decomposition theorem {#sec-complement}

The final stage is the bridge to the keystone [GEOM-06]. So far we have worked *inside* a
subspace. Now we ask how a subspace sits inside the whole space — and the answer is the
single fact least squares stands on.

::: {#def-complement}
**Definition (orthogonal complement).** Let $W$ be a subspace of $\mathbb R^n$. Its
**orthogonal complement**, written $W^\perp$ (read "$W$ perp"), is the set of all vectors
orthogonal to *every* vector in $W$:
$$
W^\perp = \{\,\mathbf z\in\mathbb R^n : \mathbf z^\top\mathbf w = 0 \text{ for all }
\mathbf w\in W\,\} .
$$ {#eq-def-complement}
:::

A picture nails it: if $W$ is a plane through the origin in $\mathbb R^3$, then $W^\perp$
is the single line through the origin perpendicular to that plane — the plane's normal
direction. If $W$ is a line, $W^\perp$ is the whole perpendicular plane. In each case $W$
and $W^\perp$ together "use up" all the directions of the space, and they share only the
origin. @fig-complement draws the plane-and-normal case.

Two quick facts make $W^\perp$ usable, and we prove both rather than wave at them.

::: {#lem-complement-basics}
**Lemma.** (a) $W^\perp$ is a subspace of $\mathbb R^n$. (b) To check that a vector
$\mathbf z$ lies in $W^\perp$, it suffices to check $\mathbf z^\top\mathbf w_i = 0$ against
a *basis* $\{\mathbf w_1,\dots,\mathbf w_m\}$ of $W$, not against every vector of $W$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**(a)** A subspace must contain $\mathbf 0$ and be closed under addition and scalar
multiplication ([GEOM-02]). First, $\mathbf 0\in W^\perp$ because
$\mathbf 0^\top\mathbf w = 0$ for all $\mathbf w$. Next, take
$\mathbf z_1,\mathbf z_2\in W^\perp$ and any scalars $a,b$. For every $\mathbf w\in W$,
linearity of the dot product gives
$$
(a\mathbf z_1 + b\mathbf z_2)^\top\mathbf w
= a\,(\mathbf z_1^\top\mathbf w) + b\,(\mathbf z_2^\top\mathbf w)
= a\cdot 0 + b\cdot 0 = 0 ,
$$
so $a\mathbf z_1 + b\mathbf z_2\in W^\perp$. Closure holds; $W^\perp$ is a subspace.

**(b)** If $\mathbf z^\top\mathbf w = 0$ for all $\mathbf w\in W$ then in particular it
holds for the basis vectors, so that direction is trivial. Conversely, suppose
$\mathbf z^\top\mathbf w_i = 0$ for each basis vector $\mathbf w_i$. Any $\mathbf w\in W$ is
some combination $\mathbf w = \sum_{i=1}^m a_i\mathbf w_i$ (that is what "basis" means), and
then
$$
\mathbf z^\top\mathbf w = \mathbf z^\top\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^m a_i\mathbf w_i\Bigr)
= \sum_{i=1}^m a_i\,(\mathbf z^\top\mathbf w_i) = \sum_{i=1}^m a_i\cdot 0 = 0 .
$$
So orthogonality to the basis propagates to orthogonality to all of $W$. Checking $m$ dot
products suffices. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Part (b) is the practical workhorse — it means "perpendicular to a plane" can be verified
against two spanning vectors instead of the infinitely many vectors of the plane, and it is
exactly the trick the normal equations of [GEOM-06] use ("residual orthogonal to every
column" rather than "to the whole column space"). Now the theorem the keystone is built on.

::: {#thm-decomposition}
**Theorem (orthogonal decomposition).** Let $W$ be a subspace of $\mathbb R^n$. Then every
vector $\mathbf x\in\mathbb R^n$ can be written in **exactly one** way as
$$
\mathbf x = \mathbf w + \mathbf z,
\qquad \mathbf w\in W,\quad \mathbf z\in W^\perp .
$$ {#eq-decomp}
The piece $\mathbf w$ is called the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf x$ onto $W$.
:::

::: {.proof}
There are two claims — *existence* (such a split is possible) and *uniqueness* (there is
only one). We prove both constructively, and the construction is exactly the coordinate
formula @eq-onb-coords, which is why this article had to come before GEOM-06.

**Existence.** Let $k = \dim W$. Choose an orthonormal basis
$\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_k\}$ of $W$. (Such a basis *exists*: any subspace has a basis
by [GEOM-03], and Gram–Schmidt, [GEOM-07], converts any basis into an orthonormal one — we
flag that machine here and build it next in the track; nothing in this proof needs its
internals, only that an orthonormal basis of $W$ exists.) Define
$$
\mathbf w = \sum_{i=1}^k (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x)\,\mathbf q_i ,
\qquad
\mathbf z = \mathbf x - \mathbf w .
$$
By construction $\mathbf w$ is a linear combination of the $\mathbf q_i$, so
$\mathbf w\in W$, and $\mathbf w + \mathbf z = \mathbf w + (\mathbf x - \mathbf w) = \mathbf x$
holds by the very definition $\mathbf z = \mathbf x - \mathbf w$. The work is showing
$\mathbf z\in W^\perp$. By @lem-complement-basics(b) it is enough to check $\mathbf z$ is
orthogonal to each basis vector $\mathbf q_j$. Compute, using linearity and then
orthonormality $\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i = \delta_{ji}$:
$$
\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf z
= \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x - \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf w
= \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x - \sum_{i=1}^k (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x)\,
  \underbrace{(\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i)}_{=\,\delta_{ji}}
= \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x - (\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x) = 0 .
$$
(The sum collapsed to its single $i=j$ term, $(\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf x)\cdot 1$, the same
probe as always.) So $\mathbf z\perp\mathbf q_j$ for every $j$, hence $\mathbf z\in W^\perp$.
The split exists.

**Uniqueness.** Suppose $\mathbf x$ had *two* such splits,
$$
\mathbf x = \mathbf w_1 + \mathbf z_1 = \mathbf w_2 + \mathbf z_2,
\qquad \mathbf w_1,\mathbf w_2\in W,\quad \mathbf z_1,\mathbf z_2\in W^\perp .
$$
Rearrange the equality $\mathbf w_1 + \mathbf z_1 = \mathbf w_2 + \mathbf z_2$ into
$$
\underbrace{\mathbf w_1 - \mathbf w_2}_{\text{call it }\mathbf d}
= \underbrace{\mathbf z_2 - \mathbf z_1}_{\text{same vector }\mathbf d} .
$$
The left side is a difference of vectors in $W$, so $\mathbf d\in W$ (a subspace is closed
under subtraction). The right side is a difference of vectors in $W^\perp$, so the *same*
vector $\mathbf d\in W^\perp$. A vector lying in both $W$ and $W^\perp$ must be orthogonal
to itself: $\mathbf d\in W^\perp$ means $\mathbf d$ is orthogonal to everything in $W$, and
$\mathbf d\in W$, so in particular $\mathbf d^\top\mathbf d = 0$. But
$\mathbf d^\top\mathbf d = \lVert\mathbf d\rVert^2$, and the only vector with zero length is
$\mathbf 0$ (from GEOM-04: $\lVert\mathbf d\rVert = 0\Rightarrow\mathbf d=\mathbf 0$). Hence
$\mathbf d = \mathbf 0$, which forces $\mathbf w_1 = \mathbf w_2$ and
$\mathbf z_1 = \mathbf z_2$. The two splits were the same; the decomposition is unique.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the theorem the rest of the series cashes in. The keystone [GEOM-06] will ask: given
a point $\mathbf x$ and a subspace $W$, what is the *closest* point of $W$ to $\mathbf x$?
@thm-decomposition already names the answer — it is $\mathbf w$, the in-$W$ piece — and
the perpendicular piece $\mathbf z$ is the *residual*, the part of $\mathbf x$ that $W$
cannot reach. GEOM-06 proves $\mathbf w$ is genuinely the nearest point (using Pythagoras,
@eq-pythagoras, on the residual) and packages the computation as the projection matrix. We
have built the foundation; the building goes up next door.

```{python}
#| label: fig-complement
#| fig-cap: "Orthogonal decomposition in $\\mathbb R^3$. The subspace $W$ is a plane through the origin (grey); its orthogonal complement $W^\\perp$ is the normal line (purple). An arbitrary vector $\\mathbf{x}$ (black) splits uniquely as $\\mathbf{x}=\\mathbf{w}+\\mathbf{z}$: the in-plane part $\\mathbf{w}\\in W$ (green) and the perpendicular part $\\mathbf{z}\\in W^\\perp$ (red), which meet $W$ at a right angle. $\\mathbf{w}$ is the closest point of the plane to $\\mathbf{x}$ — the projection the keystone GEOM-06 computes."
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
# Plane W = span(w1, w2), orthonormalized
B = rng.standard_normal((3, 2))
Qw, _ = np.linalg.qr(B)          # orthonormal basis of the plane
n = np.cross(Qw[:, 0], Qw[:, 1]) # normal direction
n = n / np.linalg.norm(n)
x = np.array([1.0, 1.3, 1.6])
w = Qw @ (Qw.T @ x)              # in-plane part
z = x - w                        # perpendicular part

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.4, 5.2))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
# draw plane patch
s = np.linspace(-1.8, 1.8, 2)
S, T = np.meshgrid(s, s)
P = (S[..., None]*Qw[:, 0] + T[..., None]*Qw[:, 1])
ax.plot_surface(P[..., 0], P[..., 1], P[..., 2], color=GREY, alpha=0.18, linewidth=0)
O = np.zeros(3)
ax.quiver(*O, *x, color="black", lw=2.6, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
ax.quiver(*O, *w, color=ACCENT, lw=2.4, arrow_length_ratio=0.1)
ax.quiver(*w, *z, color=ARROW, lw=2.2, arrow_length_ratio=0.15)
ax.quiver(*O, *(1.7*n), color=PURPLE, lw=2.0, arrow_length_ratio=0.12, alpha=0.9)
ax.text(*(x*1.04), r"$\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=13)
ax.text(*(w*1.05 + [0, 0, -0.15]), r"$\mathbf{w}\in W$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.text(*((w + z*0.5) + [0.12, 0.0, 0.0]), r"$\mathbf{z}\in W^\perp$", color=ARROW, fontsize=12)
ax.text(*(1.75*n), r"$W^\perp$", color=PURPLE, fontsize=12)
ax.text(1.4, -1.6, -0.2, r"$W$", color=GREY, fontsize=13)
ax.set_xlim(-1.8, 1.8); ax.set_ylim(-1.8, 1.8); ax.set_zlim(-1.2, 2.0)
ax.set_xlabel("axis 1"); ax.set_ylabel("axis 2"); ax.set_zlabel("axis 3")
ax.set_title("$\\mathbf{x} = \\mathbf{w} + \\mathbf{z}$, the unique split")
ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"w . z  (should be 0, w perp z) : {w @ z:.2e}")
print(f"||x||^2                         : {x @ x:.4f}")
print(f"||w||^2 + ||z||^2  (Pythagoras) : {w @ w + z @ z:.4f}")
```

The printout under @fig-complement is worth a sentence: it verifies $\mathbf w^\top\mathbf z
= 0$ (the two pieces really are perpendicular) and that
$\lVert\mathbf x\rVert^2 = \lVert\mathbf w\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf z\rVert^2$ — the
decomposition obeys Pythagoras, because $\mathbf w\perp\mathbf z$. The split and the
Pythagorean theorem are the same geometry seen from two angles.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on real, honest data — including one case at the edge, where
"orthonormal" is the wrong thing to demand and the technique strains.

## Example 1 — orthonormal coordinates beat a skewed basis, in numbers {#sec-ex-skewed}

The abstract claim of @sec-coords is that an orthonormal basis turns a coordinate-finding
*linear solve* into a list of dot products. Let us watch that happen on a concrete plane in
$\mathbb R^4$ and confirm the two routes agree.

Take a subspace $W\subset\mathbb R^4$ spanned by two genuinely non-perpendicular vectors —
say two correlated feature directions. We want the coordinates of a vector $\mathbf x\in W$
in (a) the original skewed basis, which requires solving a $2\times2$ system, and (b) an
orthonormal basis of the *same* plane, which requires only dot products.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-skewed
#| fig-cap: "Two bases for the same plane $W\\subset\\mathbb R^4$. The skewed basis $\\{\\mathbf{v}_1,\\mathbf{v}_2\\}$ requires solving a $2\\times2$ linear system to recover coordinates; the orthonormal basis $\\{\\mathbf{q}_1,\\mathbf{q}_2\\}$ (built by QR from the same two vectors) recovers them with two dot products via @eq-onb-coords. The bar chart shows that the *reconstructed* vector is identical either way — the geometry is the same; only the bookkeeping cost differs. The printout confirms both routes return $\\mathbf{x}$ exactly."
# A skewed (non-orthogonal) basis of a plane in R^4
v1 = np.array([2.0, 1.0, 0.0, 1.0])
v2 = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 1.0, 0.0])
V = np.column_stack([v1, v2])

# A target vector that lives in the plane: x = 1.5 v1 - 0.7 v2
true_coeffs = np.array([1.5, -0.7])
x = V @ true_coeffs

# Route A: skewed basis -> solve the 2x2 normal-style system (V^T V) c = V^T x
A = V.T @ V
c_skew = np.linalg.solve(A, V.T @ x)     # coupled solve

# Route B: orthonormal basis of the SAME plane via QR (Gram-Schmidt under the hood)
Q, _ = np.linalg.qr(V)                    # Q columns span the same plane, orthonormal
c_onb = Q.T @ x                           # just dot products, no system

recon_skew = V @ c_skew
recon_onb = Q @ c_onb

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
idx = np.arange(4)
width = 0.27
ax.bar(idx - width, x, width, color=POINT, label=r"$\mathbf{x}$ (target)")
ax.bar(idx, recon_skew, width, color=ARROW, label="from skewed coords (solve)")
ax.bar(idx + width, recon_onb, width, color=ACCENT, label="from ON coords (dot products)")
ax.set_xticks(idx); ax.set_xticklabels([f"comp {i+1}" for i in idx])
ax.set_ylabel("value"); ax.set_title("Same reconstruction, two bookkeeping costs")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("Skewed basis (need a 2x2 solve):")
print(f"  V^T V = \n{np.round(A, 3)}    <- off-diagonal {A[0,1]:.2f} != 0, basis is coupled")
print(f"  coords c_skew = {np.round(c_skew, 4)}   reconstruction err = {np.linalg.norm(recon_skew - x):.2e}")
print("Orthonormal basis (just dot products):")
print(f"  Q^T Q = \n{np.round(Q.T @ Q, 6)}   <- identity, basis is decoupled")
print(f"  coords c_onb  = {np.round(c_onb, 4)}   reconstruction err = {np.linalg.norm(recon_onb - x):.2e}")
```

Read the two printouts side by side. For the skewed basis, $V^\top V$ has a nonzero
off-diagonal entry — that number *is* the cross-talk between $\mathbf v_1$ and
$\mathbf v_2$, and it is exactly why route A had to solve the system
$(V^\top V)\mathbf c = V^\top\mathbf x$ rather than read coordinates off directly. For the
orthonormal basis, $Q^\top Q = I$ — no off-diagonal, no cross-talk — so route B's
coordinates are literally the entries of $Q^\top\mathbf x$, no solve. Both routes
reconstruct $\mathbf x$ to machine precision, because they describe the *same* plane; the
only difference is the labor and the numerical conditioning. In two dimensions the saving
is invisible; in the hundreds of dimensions of a real design matrix it is the difference
between a stable, fast computation and an ill-conditioned one ([GEOM-26]).

## Example 2 — a real orthonormal basis you use every day: the DCT and image patches {#sec-ex-dct}

Here is an orthonormal basis that powers a technology in your pocket. JPEG compression
slices an image into $8\times8$ blocks of pixels and re-expresses each block in the
**discrete cosine transform (DCT)** basis — a set of $64$ fixed "cosine pattern" vectors
that are *mutually orthogonal*. The whole reason JPEG can compute each coefficient with one
dot product (and then throw the small ones away) is the orthonormal-coordinate formula
@eq-onb-coords. We will build a $1$-D version, which is honest and fully visible, and verify
the orthonormality and the coordinate formula directly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-dct
#| fig-cap: "The DCT-II basis for signals of length $N=8$ — eight mutually orthogonal cosine patterns of increasing frequency (top, scaled to unit length). **Bottom left:** the Gram matrix $Q^\\top Q$ of the eight basis vectors is the identity to machine precision (white = 1 on the diagonal, near-black = 0 off it): they form an *orthonormal* basis. **Bottom right:** a sample signal (black) and its reconstruction (green dashed) from coordinates computed purely as dot products $c_i=\\mathbf{q}_i^\\top\\mathbf{x}$ — exact, no linear solve. This is JPEG's coordinate step in one dimension."
N = 8
# DCT-II basis vectors, normalized to unit length (an orthonormal basis of R^8)
Q = np.zeros((N, N))
for k in range(N):
    col = np.cos(np.pi * (np.arange(N) + 0.5) * k / N)
    Q[:, k] = col / np.linalg.norm(col)

# A sample signal (a smooth ramp + a wiggle) and its DCT coordinates
xsig = np.array([3.0, 3.4, 3.9, 4.1, 3.6, 2.8, 2.3, 2.5])
coords = Q.T @ xsig               # coordinates = dot products
recon = Q @ coords                # reconstruct

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.0, 6.2))
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 2, height_ratios=[1.1, 1.0])

# top: the basis patterns
axt = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, :])
for k in range(N):
    axt.plot(np.arange(N), Q[:, k] + 0.0*k, marker="o", ms=3, lw=1.4, alpha=0.9,
             label=f"freq {k}")
axt.set_title("The eight orthonormal DCT basis vectors (cosine patterns)")
axt.set_xlabel("sample index"); axt.set_ylabel("amplitude")
axt.legend(ncol=4, fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

# bottom left: Gram matrix
axg = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0])
im = axg.imshow(Q.T @ Q, cmap="gray_r", vmin=0, vmax=1)
axg.set_title(r"$Q^\top Q = I$ (orthonormal)")
axg.set_xlabel("j"); axg.set_ylabel("i")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axg, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

# bottom right: signal + reconstruction
axs = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1])
axs.plot(np.arange(N), xsig, color="black", marker="o", lw=2.0, label="signal $\\mathbf{x}$")
axs.plot(np.arange(N), recon, color=ACCENT, ls="--", marker="s", ms=4, lw=1.8,
         label="reconstructed")
axs.set_title("Reconstruct from dot-product coords")
axs.set_xlabel("sample index"); axs.set_ylabel("value")
axs.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"max |Q^T Q - I|              : {np.abs(Q.T @ Q - np.eye(N)).max():.2e}  (orthonormal)")
print(f"reconstruction error         : {np.linalg.norm(recon - xsig):.2e}  (exact)")
print(f"||x||^2                       : {xsig @ xsig:.4f}")
print(f"sum of coords^2 (Parseval)    : {coords @ coords:.4f}")
print(f"DCT coordinates               : {np.round(coords, 3)}")
```

Three things to notice, each a theorem from @sec-formal made visible. The Gram matrix
$Q^\top Q$ is the identity (the cosine patterns are orthonormal — @def-orth-set), so the
coordinates come from dot products with *no solve* (@thm-onb-coords), and the squared
length of the signal equals the sum of the squared coordinates (Parseval, @eq-parseval —
the two energy numbers in the printout match). The compression payoff, which belongs to a
signal-processing course rather than this article, is that for natural images most of those
$64$ coordinates are tiny and can be coarsely quantized or dropped — but *that we can read,
zero, and reconstruct each coordinate independently* is purely the orthonormality we proved
here.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: when orthogonality is the wrong demand {#sec-ex-edge}

An honest article shows where the tool strains. Orthonormal bases are not free, and they
are not always what you want. Here is the most important honest caveat, made concrete.

Consider two real data features that are **strongly correlated** — say height in
centimetres and height in inches recorded with slight rounding, or two near-duplicate
sensor channels. As directions in the space of observations they point *almost the same
way*: the angle between them is tiny, far from $90^\circ$. You might be tempted to
orthogonalize them (Gram–Schmidt) to get the computational comfort we have been praising.
Watch what happens.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge: orthogonalizing nearly-parallel directions is numerically violent. Two feature vectors $\\mathbf{a},\\mathbf{b}$ point almost the same way (angle $\\approx 1.4^\\circ$, left). Gram–Schmidt subtracts $\\mathbf{a}$'s shadow from $\\mathbf{b}$ to make the second orthonormal vector $\\mathbf{q}_2$ (right, purple): because the shadow is almost all of $\\mathbf{b}$, $\\mathbf{q}_2$ is the *tiny leftover difference*, hugely amplified to unit length. Its direction is dominated by noise/rounding in the data — the orthonormal basis exists, but $\\mathbf{q}_2$ is meaningless. This is the geometric face of multicollinearity and ill-conditioning."
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
base = np.array([3.0, 2.0])
a = base + np.array([0.0, 0.0])
b = base * 1.0 + np.array([0.10, 0.18])     # nearly parallel to a
ang = np.degrees(np.arccos((a @ b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))))

# Gram-Schmidt: q1 from a, q2 from b minus its shadow on q1
q1 = a / np.linalg.norm(a)
b_shadow = (q1 @ b) * q1
b_perp = b - b_shadow
q2 = b_perp / np.linalg.norm(b_perp)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.2))
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 3.8); ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.8)

axL.annotate("", xy=a, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.4))
axL.annotate("", xy=b, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.4))
axL.text(*(a + [0.05, -0.2]), r"$\mathbf{a}$", color=ARROW, fontsize=13)
axL.text(*(b + [0.05, 0.12]), r"$\mathbf{b}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
axL.set_title(f"Two near-parallel features (angle {ang:.1f}$^\\circ$)")

axR.annotate("", xy=q1, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ARROW, lw=2.4))
axR.annotate("", xy=b, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GREY, lw=1.6))
axR.annotate("", xy=b, xytext=b_shadow, arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=PURPLE, lw=1.6))
axR.annotate("", xy=q2, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=PURPLE, lw=2.6))
axR.text(*(q1 + [0.05, -0.22]), r"$\mathbf{q}_1$", color=ARROW, fontsize=12)
axR.text(*(q2 + [-0.5, 0.08]), r"$\mathbf{q}_2$ (amplified leftover)", color=PURPLE, fontsize=10)
axR.text(*(b + [0.05, 0.12]), r"$\mathbf{b}$", color=GREY, fontsize=12)
axR.set_title("Gram–Schmidt: $\\mathbf{q}_2$ is the fragile remainder")
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"angle between a and b           : {ang:.2f} degrees (far from 90)")
print(f"||b - shadow|| (the leftover)   : {np.linalg.norm(b_perp):.4f}  (tiny)")
print(f"||b||                            : {np.linalg.norm(b):.4f}")
print(f"q2 = leftover / ||leftover||, so a small perturbation of b swings q2's direction wildly")
```

The orthonormal basis $\{\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2\}$ *exists* — the theorems of this article
never lied. But $\mathbf q_2$ was manufactured by subtracting almost all of $\mathbf b$ away
and then blowing the tiny remainder up to unit length. A whisker of measurement noise in
$\mathbf b$ would point that remainder in an entirely different direction. So the
orthonormal coordinates of any vector along $\mathbf q_2$ are numerically untrustworthy.

**The honest lesson, and what you reach for instead.** Orthonormality is a property of the
*basis you choose*, not of the data, and forcing it onto nearly-dependent directions does
not create information that was not there — it just relocates the instability into one
fragile basis vector. When features are near-collinear, the right responses are not "make
them orthogonal and proceed" but: **regularize** (ridge regression adds $\lambda I$ to
stabilize the solve — [REGR-07], [REGR-14]), **drop or combine** the redundant feature, or
use the **SVD** ([GEOM-20], [GEOM-21]), which exposes exactly which directions are
well-determined and which are noise by their singular values, and lets you discard the
fragile ones honestly. The condition number that quantifies all of this is [GEOM-26]. The
orthonormal-basis machinery is golden when the directions you orthogonalize are genuinely
distinct; it is a trap when they are not, and a good practitioner checks the angle (or the
singular values) before trusting the result.

# Failure modes and boundaries {#sec-failure}

Collecting the honest edges, several of which the examples already exposed.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**An orthonormal basis exists for any subspace, but computing one is not always
trustworthy.** Three traps, in order of how often they bite:

1. **Near-collinear directions (Example 3).** Orthogonalizing nearly-parallel vectors
   produces a legitimate orthonormal basis whose later vectors are dominated by noise. The
   basis is mathematically valid and numerically worthless. Diagnose with the angle between
   the vectors, or — at scale — the condition number / singular values ([GEOM-26],
   [GEOM-20]). Fix by regularizing, dropping features, or using the SVD.
2. **"Orthogonal" $\neq$ "uncorrelated" unless you centre first.** In statistics, two data
   vectors are *uncorrelated* when their **mean-centred** versions are orthogonal, not the
   raw vectors. Two all-positive features can have a large dot product (hence look far from
   orthogonal) yet be statistically uncorrelated once their means are removed. Know which
   space you are in — raw $\mathbb R^n$ or the mean-centred subspace — before reading
   "orthogonal" as "unrelated." (Covariance geometry is Probability's to own; the warning
   is geometric.)
3. **Orthonormal $\neq$ orthogonal.** An *orthogonal set* only requires perpendicularity;
   an *orthonormal set* also requires unit length. The coordinate formula
   $c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$ needs the *normal* part — for a merely orthogonal
   (not normalized) basis the formula is $c_i = (\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf x)/\lVert\mathbf
   u_i\rVert^2$, with the extra denominator. Forgetting the normalization silently rescales
   every coordinate.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is it worth converting to an orthonormal basis, and when is it a waste?"* Convert
when you will **reuse** the basis for many coordinate computations or projections (a fixed
DCT/Fourier basis, a fixed orthonormal basis of a model's column space) — the one-time
Gram–Schmidt/QR cost is amortized and every subsequent coordinate is a cheap, stable dot
product. Do **not** bother when (a) you need coordinates in one specific, meaningful
non-orthogonal basis (the original features carry interpretation that orthogonalizing
destroys), or (b) the directions are near-collinear, where orthogonalization manufactures a
fragile basis (Example 3). And note that for the single most common task — projecting onto
a subspace for least squares — you do not *need* an explicit orthonormal basis at all; the
normal equations of [GEOM-06] work in the original basis, and an orthonormal basis is the
*optional* upgrade that makes the projection $P = QQ^\top$ instead of
$P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$. Cheaper and stabler, but a choice, not a requirement.
:::

One more boundary worth stating plainly, because it is a common source of confusion. The
decomposition theorem @thm-decomposition is about **orthogonal** complements with respect
to the **standard** dot product. If you change the inner product — weight some coordinates
more than others, as generalized least squares does with a covariance matrix ([REGR-11],
[GEOM-19]) — then "orthogonal" changes meaning, and the perpendicular complement rotates
with it. The geometry of this article is correct for *whatever* inner product you fix at
the start, but you must fix one; "orthogonal" is always *orthogonal with respect to a chosen
inner product*, never an absolute.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*]**, which gave length, angle, and the relation
$\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = \lVert\mathbf x\rVert\lVert\mathbf y\rVert\cos\theta$. This
article followed the special case $\theta = 90^\circ$ to three results — Pythagoras,
orthonormal coordinates as dot products, and the unique orthogonal decomposition. From
here:

- **[GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*]** — the keystone. It takes the in-$W$
  piece $\mathbf w$ of @thm-decomposition, proves it is the *nearest* point of $W$ to
  $\mathbf x$ (via Pythagoras on the residual), and builds the projection matrix. Least
  squares ([REGR-04], [REGR-05]) *is* this projection.
- **[GEOM-07 *Gram–Schmidt and the QR Decomposition*]** — the machine that builds the
  orthonormal bases we assumed exist here, one projection at a time, and the $A = QR$
  factorization that bookkeeps it. The existence step of @thm-decomposition is discharged
  in full there.
- **[GEOM-16 *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*]** — the deepest payoff: a real
  symmetric matrix has an *orthonormal* eigenbasis, so the spectral decomposition
  $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ inherits every gift of this article. PCA, quadratic forms, and
  second-order optimization all live in that orthonormal eigenbasis.
- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — every matrix maps one orthonormal
  basis to another, scaled; the SVD is that statement made into a factorization. The
  near-collinear failure of Example 3 is read off its singular values.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The historical thread.** Fourier's 1822 *Théorie analytique de la chaleur* is the
  origin of "compute each coefficient by an independent integral against the basis
  function" — the coordinate formula @eq-onb-coords in an infinite-dimensional inner-product
  space. Gram (1883) and Schmidt (1907) gave the orthogonalization process that [GEOM-07]
  builds; the abstraction to general inner-product (Hilbert) spaces is Hilbert and von
  Neumann's, where Parseval's identity @eq-parseval becomes the defining feature of an
  orthonormal basis.
- **The one idea to carry forward.** *Orthogonality turns geometry into arithmetic by
  killing cross terms.* Every theorem here — independence, Pythagoras, dot-product
  coordinates, unique decomposition — is the same observation: when directions meet at right
  angles, the term $\mathbf u_i^\top\mathbf u_j$ vanishes for $i\neq j$, and a tangle of
  coupled quantities falls apart into independent ones. Keep that single mechanism in view
  and the rest of the linear-algebra track stops being a list of tricks.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do we actually manufacture an orthonormal
  basis from a given one?* — Gram–Schmidt, [GEOM-07]. *How do we compute the nearest point in
  a subspace, and is it really nearest?* — projection, [GEOM-06]. *Which matrices come with a
  ready-made orthonormal basis attached?* — the symmetric ones, [GEOM-16]. *What is the
  right orthonormal basis for a non-standard (weighted) inner product?* — generalized least
  squares and whitening, [REGR-11] and [GEOM-19].
